title: Generic Tools, Borescopes, Fiberscopes, and Video Borescopes
narrationScript: This lesson is about the hardware you will actually hold. We start with the humble magnifier and mirror, then work through the three scope families, rigid borescopes, fiberscopes, and video borescopes, and finish with how to choose among them. First the generic tools. An inspection magnifier is a converging lens used inside its focal length, and its power is roughly two hundred fifty millimeters divided by the focal length. Field work mostly uses five to ten power. Here is the trade you cannot escape. More power means a smaller field of view and a shorter working distance. A ten power loupe shows you a patch maybe fifteen to twenty millimeters wide from a couple of centimeters away. So you scan with the unaided eye first, then bring in the magnifier to interrogate what you found. Hold it so the surface stays lit. Your own shadow is the usual enemy. The inspection mirror is the other everyday tool. It folds your line of sight around obstructions, into bores, behind flanges. Remember the direct viewing geometry. Codes generally want the eye within six hundred millimeters of the surface and the line of sight at not less than thirty degrees to it, and a mirror is the standard way to satisfy that geometry when straight viewing cannot. Mind that the mirror image is reversed left to right, keep the glass clean and unscratched, and add a flashlight at a low angle when you need surface relief to stand out. When the path to the surface is too small or too deep for mirrors, you move to borescopes. A rigid borescope is a straight tube containing the lens train from the previous lesson, with an eyepiece or camera at your end and fiber light guides wrapped around the optics to deliver illumination. Because the image rides on precision glass the whole way, a good rigid scope gives the best image of any scope family. Diameters run from about a millimeter up to around sixteen, and lengths commonly reach a meter or somewhat more. The catch is in the name. It is rigid. It needs straight line access, and it is surprisingly fragile. Drop it or pry with it and the relay train misaligns for good. You also choose the direction of view when you pick the scope. Forward for looking down a bore, side at ninety degrees for bore walls, forward oblique around forty five degrees for corners and chamfers, and retro for looking back toward the entry point, think valve seats and the back side of openings. And remember that magnification changes with distance. Closer is bigger. Never judge size through a scope without a reference. A fiberscope packages a coherent image bundle in a flexible insertion tube, with a focusing objective at the tip, an eyepiece at your end, and control cables that articulate the tip in two or four directions. It goes around corners that stop a rigid scope cold. The cost is the image. You are looking at a mosaic of fiber ends, so the picture is honeycombed, resolution is set by fiber count, and every broken fiber is a dead black pixel forever. Treat the bundle gently, respect the minimum bend radius, relax the articulation before you withdraw, and never force the tip. The video borescope replaces the image bundle with a camera chip right at the tip. The image becomes an electrical signal immediately, so length stops mattering to image quality. Working lengths run to tens of meters. Illumination comes from bright LEDs at the tip or a light box through fibers. The display end gives you freeze frame, recording, annotation, and on many units on screen measurement. Image quality now rivals or beats fiberscopes at most diameters, which is why video probes dominate new purchases. They cost more, and the tip electronics set temperature limits, but for most remote work they are the default choice. Beyond the standard families sit the special purpose scopes. Ultraviolet scopes carry UV light for fluorescent penetrant examination inside assemblies. High temperature scopes wear cooling jackets for furnaces and running engines. Waterproof and explosion rated designs handle flooded systems and hazardous atmospheres, and swing prism scopes change their direction of view without changing the instrument. Choosing comes down to three questions. What is the access, straight or curved, how far, through what opening. What must you resolve, because a half millimeter crack demands far more image than a missing bolt check. And what are the conditions, temperature, fluids, atmosphere. Straight access with the highest detail says rigid scope. Curved access says fiberscope or, almost always now, a video probe. Long runs, documentation, and measurement say video probe without argument. Match the tool to the job, and verify resolution at the working distance before you call anything good.

sections.heading: Generic Tools: Magnifiers and Mirrors
sections.body:
The cheapest tools in the kit still solve most close-access problems.

**Magnifiers.** An inspection magnifier is a converging lens used inside its focal length; power ≈ 250 mm ÷ focal length. Field VT lives at **5×–10×**; above 10× the field of view and working distance shrink so much that hand-held use becomes impractical — a 10× loupe shows a patch roughly 15–20 mm wide from about 25 mm away. Standard technique: locate candidate indications with the unaided eye, then characterize them under the magnifier. Prefer corrected doublet or triplet loupes; single-element lenses fringe and distort at the edges. Watch the lighting — your head and the lens housing both cast shadows, which is why many loupes carry built-in LED rings.

**Mirrors.** The inspection mirror folds the sight line around obstructions — behind flanges, into bores, under components. Direct visual examination geometry still applies: codes commonly require the eye within **600 mm (24 in.)** of the surface with a viewing angle **not less than 30°** to it, and a mirror is the accepted way to achieve that geometry when straight viewing cannot. Practical points:

- The mirror image is **laterally reversed** — orient yourself before mapping indication locations.
- Mirror condition matters: scratches, corrosion spots, and fogging become false indications or hide real ones.
- Pair the mirror with an angled flashlight so raking light pops out relief such as undercut and pitting.

Telescoping and articulating stems, and head sizes from fingernail to palm, cover most geometries.

sections.caption: Magnifier power trades directly against field of view, and an inspection mirror folds the sight line to restore code-required viewing geometry behind obstructions.

sections.heading: Rigid Borescopes
sections.body:
A **rigid borescope** is a straight optical tube: objective at the distal tip, relay lens train down the bore, eyepiece or camera coupling at the proximal end, and a sheath of illumination fibers surrounding the optics, fed from a light source. Because the image travels on precision glass the whole way, **a quality rigid scope delivers the best image of any scope family** — the natural choice when fine detail must be resolved through straight access: gun-drilled passages, heat-exchanger tubes, cylinder bores, machined ports.

Key selection parameters:

- **Diameter:** about 1 mm (needle scopes) up to roughly 16 mm — the scope must clear the access hole with room to manipulate.
- **Length:** commonly 150 mm to about 1.5 m; brightness drops as trains lengthen.
- **Direction of view (DOV):** forward 0°, forward-oblique about 45°, side 90°, retro about 110–120° — fixed at purchase, or variable on swing-prism models.
- **Field of view:** typically 40°–90°; wider fields show more area at lower apparent magnification.
- **Focus:** fixed focus with generous depth of field, or a focusing eyepiece.

Magnification is not fixed — it increases as tip-to-target distance shrinks, which is why sizing anything through a scope requires a reference or a measuring system. Handle rigid scopes like the precision optics they are: no prying, no side-loads, no drops. A bent tube misaligns the relay train permanently.

sections.caption: Direction of view is built into the scope tip — forward, forward-oblique, side, or retro — and field of view sets how much you see at once.

sections.heading: Fiberscopes
sections.body:
A **fiberscope** packages the coherent image bundle from the previous lesson in a flexible insertion tube. At the tip: a small objective lens focusing the scene onto the bundle face, beside the light-guide outlets. At your end: a focusing eyepiece and **articulation controls** — cables that deflect the tip in two or four directions, typically 120° or more, steering both the view and the path.

Fiberscopes go where rigid scopes cannot: through curved passages, multiple bends, and offset cavities — combustion chambers via spark-plug ports, turbine internals, pipe bends. Diameters run from about 0.6 mm ultrafine to about 13 mm; working lengths reach several meters.

The limitations are inherent to the bundle:

- **Pixelated (honeycomb) image** — resolution is set by fiber count, not lens quality, and fine cracks can hide between the fiber 'pixels'.
- **Broken fibers** accumulate as permanent black dots; written practices limit how many, and how clustered, before the scope is unserviceable.
- **Light and contrast fall off with length** compared with a similar rigid scope.

Handling discipline keeps them alive: respect the **minimum bend radius**, never crush or pinch the insertion tube, relax (neutralize) articulation before withdrawing, and store the scope hung or loosely coiled. A fiberscope abused once carries its black-dot scars for the rest of its service life.

sections.heading: Video Borescopes and Special-Purpose Scopes
sections.body:
A **video borescope (videoprobe)** puts the camera at the tip: a miniature CCD/CMOS sensor behind the objective converts the image to an electrical signal immediately, so it travels up the insertion tube as wiring, not optics. The consequences define modern remote VT:

- **Length no longer degrades the image** — working lengths of 10–30 m are routine.
- **Bright LED illumination** at the tip (or fiber-delivered light) is adjusted from the handset.
- The handset adds **freeze, still and video recording, annotation, split-screen comparison, and on-screen measurement** (next lesson).
- **Servo or cable articulation** steers the tip; interchangeable optical tip adapters change DOV, field of view, and depth of field in seconds.

Image quality equals or beats fiberscopes at most diameters from about 2 mm up, making videoprobes the default for remote work — at higher cost, and with tip-electronics temperature limits (commonly around 60–80 °C continuous unless cooled).

**Special-purpose scopes** adapt these families to hostile niches: **UV borescopes** deliver ultraviolet light for in-place fluorescent penetrant examination inside assemblies; **high-temperature scopes** wear air- or water-cooling jackets for furnaces and running engines; **waterproof and submersible scopes** handle flooded systems; **explosion-proof or intrinsically safe** designs enter fuel tanks and hazardous atmospheres; **swing-prism scopes** vary direction of view continuously; and **micro-borescopes** under 1 mm reach fuel nozzles and cooling holes.

sections.caption: Three scope families compared by image path: glass lens train (rigid), aligned fiber bundle (fiberscope), and tip-mounted sensor with electronic signal (video borescope).
Selecting among the three scope families.
rows: High, independent of length; supports measurement
Fragile; bending ruins the relay train

sections.title: Three questions pick the scope
sections.body:
1. **Access** — straight or curved? How deep? Through what opening diameter?
2. **Resolution** — what is the smallest discontinuity you must detect, and will the procedure ask you to demonstrate it on a target?
3. **Environment** — temperature, fluids, atmosphere, UV requirement?

Straight access plus finest detail → rigid scope. Curved access → video probe, or fiberscope where diameter or budget dictates. Long runs plus documentation and measurement → video probe, no argument.

checkQuestions.question: You must examine the full wall of a straight, 25 mm diameter gun-drilled oil passage, 400 mm deep, for fine machining tears. The best instrument is…
options: A 10× magnifier with an inspection mirror
A rigid borescope with a side (90°) direction of view
A pocket flashlight and unaided eye
checkQuestions.explanation: Straight access plus the finest detail requirement points to a rigid scope, and examining the bore wall calls for a side direction of view. A fiberscope's honeycomb image could hide fine tears.

checkQuestions.question: The honeycomb texture seen in a fiberscope image is caused by…
options: The pixel grid of the camera sensor
The mosaic of individual fiber ends in the coherent bundle
checkQuestions.explanation: You are literally viewing the bundle's fiber ends — each fiber is one picture element, so the fiber mosaic shows as a honeycomb and sets the resolution limit.

checkQuestions.question: The fundamental difference between a video borescope and a fiberscope is that the video borescope…
options: Uses an incoherent bundle to carry the image
Converts the image to an electrical signal with a sensor at the tip
checkQuestions.explanation: With a CCD/CMOS chip at the tip, the image travels as electrons in wire instead of light in fibers — so image quality no longer depends on insertion-tube length.

checkQuestions.question: An inspection mirror lets you examine a weld behind an obstruction as a direct visual examination. Codes typically still require…
options: The eye within 600 mm along the line of sight and a viewing angle not less than 30° to the surface
A minimum of 2000 lux at the mirror face
checkQuestions.explanation: The mirror folds the sight line, but the direct-examination geometry — about 600 mm eye-to-surface and at least a 30° viewing angle — still has to be met along that folded path.

checkQuestions.question: Which special-purpose scope is appropriate for verifying fluorescent penetrant indications inside an assembled gearbox?
checkQuestions.explanation: Fluorescent penetrant requires ultraviolet excitation; a UV borescope delivers UV light and views the fluorescing indications in place, without disassembly.

references: ASME Section V, Article 9 — direct and remote visual examination geometry (generic reference)
Typical manufacturer specifications for borescopes, fiberscopes, and videoprobes

simulator.title: RVI Trainer: Compressor Blade Inspection
simulator.intro: Borescope inspection of a compressor stage. Index blade by blade — engine inspections fail audits when technicians skip blades. Find every damaged blade.

targets.label: Leading-edge nick
targets.feedback: Foreign object damage on the leading edge — measure against the engine manual's serviceable limits before condemning.

targets.label: Tip curl
targets.feedback: Tip damage — check the adjacent blades and downstream stages; FOD rarely hits just one blade.
